coredata.dc:title: Tailoring multifunctional gel for sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection and effective adsorption of cadmium(Ⅱ) and lead(Ⅱ) ions in water
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Shuangquan
Jin, Yong
Shi, Liangjie
Li, Yupeng
Zhou, Rong
coredata.dc:description: Water contamination caused by toxic cadmium (Cd2+) and lead (Pb2+) has become a global environmental issue, making it imperative to develop versatile functional materials for their monitoring and remediation. However, developing a versatile platform for the simultaneous sensitive multiple signal detection and effective removal of Cd2+ and Pb2+ remains a formidable challenge. Herein, a multifunctional gel for sensitive detection and effective adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+ is facilely constructed by integrating glycidyl methacrylate functionalized fluorescent graphene quantum dots and N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide co-crosslinked poly(thioctic acid) with physical crosslinked fluorescent gelatin. Interestingly, the gel shows cyan to chartreuse/violet fluorescence color change and fluorescent turn-on/turn-off switch after adsorbing Cd2+/Pb2+, thus for the first time simultaneous sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ are achieved in a broad liner range (0.02–0.24 μmol). Meanwhile, the gel can effectively absorb Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water with maximum adsorption capacities of 194.553 and 259.067 mg g−1, respectively, surpassing previously reported adsorbents. Additionally, the gel exhibits excellent self-healing capability, reprocessability, biodegradability and biocompatibility, which can prolong its service life and prevent secondary contamination in practical applications. This study not only develops a versatile platform for the simultaneous sensitive detection and effective removal of Cd2+ and Pb2+, but also provides an innovative design strategy for inspiring future research to fabricate multifunctional materials for monitoring and decontamination heavy metal polluted water.

head.ce:title: Tailoring multifunctional gel for sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection and effective adsorption of cadmium(Ⅱ) and lead(Ⅱ) ions in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Leather Chemistry and Engineering, Ministry of Education, National Engineering Research Center of Clean Technology in Leather Industry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Leather Chemistry and Engineering
National Engineering Research Center of Clean Technology in Leather Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Leather Chemistry and Engineering, Ministry of Education, National Engineering Research Center of Clean Technology in Leather Industry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water contamination caused by toxic cadmium (Cd2+) and lead (Pb2+) has become a global environmental issue, making it imperative to develop versatile functional materials for their monitoring and remediation. However, developing a versatile platform for the simultaneous sensitive multiple signal detection and effective removal of Cd2+ and Pb2+ remains a formidable challenge. Herein, a multifunctional gel for sensitive detection and effective adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+ is facilely constructed by integrating glycidyl methacrylate functionalized fluorescent graphene quantum dots and N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide co-crosslinked poly(thioctic acid) with physical crosslinked fluorescent gelatin. Interestingly, the gel shows cyan to chartreuse/violet fluorescence color change and fluorescent turn-on/turn-off switch after adsorbing Cd2+/Pb2+, thus for the first time simultaneous sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ are achieved in a broad liner range (0.02–0.24 μmol). Meanwhile, the gel can effectively absorb Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water with maximum adsorption capacities of 194.553 and 259.067 mg g−1, respectively, surpassing previously reported adsorbents. Additionally, the gel exhibits excellent self-healing capability, reprocessability, biodegradability and biocompatibility, which can prolong its service life and prevent secondary contamination in practical applications. This study not only develops a versatile platform for the simultaneous sensitive detection and effective removal of Cd2+ and Pb2+, but also provides an innovative design strategy for inspiring future research to fabricate multifunctional materials for monitoring and decontamination heavy metal polluted water.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination caused by heavy metal ions (Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr6+) poses serious public health threat due to their toxicity, nonbiodegradability and carcinogenicity characteristics [1], making it imperative to explore and develop versatile materials for venomous metal ions detection and adsorption. However, most developed materials can only perform either detection [2–4] or adsorption [5–7] task separately at present, which severely limits their practical applications. Accordingly, integrating detection and adsorption functions into a functional material to achieve simultaneous detection and adsorption of toxic heavy metal ions is highly attractive and desired. To date, mesoporous nanocomposites [8,9], metal/covalent-organic frameworks (MOFs/COFs) [10,11], magnetic nanoparticles [12], synthetic polymers [13,14], aggregation-induced emission materials [15] and hydrogels [16–18] have been developed for simultaneous detection and adsorption of toxic metal ions. Among these materials, hydrogels with flexible synthesis procedures, excellent hydrophilicity, three-dimensional porous structure and rich chelating groups have attracted special attention. However, most of the developed hydrogels are aimed at Hg2+ or Cr6+, while the attentions to Cd2+ and Pb2+ are far from enough..

Recently, some hydrogels with monochromatic fluorescence properties were achieved only for simultaneous detection and adsorption of Pb2+ by loading a signal “turn off” fluorophore into a hydrogel matrix [19,20]. Nevertheless, monochromatic fluorescence usually suffers from the limitations of poor selectivity and false-positive results, and that monotonic fluorescence intensity changes during sensing process is difficult to distinguish with naked-eye [21]. Hydrogel with naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection ability can meet the requirements on-site and laboratory detection. In addition to the detection ability, the adsorption capacities of the developed hydrogels toward heavy metal ions are also highly concerned. With hydroxyl on PVA/agarose as active sites, the adsorption capacities of these two hydrogels were 0.0616 mg g−1 [19] and 62.5 mg g−1 [20], respectively, which are far beyond satisfactory. According to literatures, the introduction of more functional groups, such as carboxyl [5], amino [6], thiol [7] with a stronger chelation ability into the hydrogel is critical to enhance its adsorption capacity. However, the adsorption capacities of these developed hydrogels for Cd2+ and Pb2+ are still not high enough. Therefore, how to design a hydrogel to achieve high adsorption capacities for Cd2+ and Pb2+ remains a great challenge. Therefore, developing ratiometric fluorescent sensing hydrogels based on dual fluorophores, which can achieve simultaneous sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection and effective adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+, is more attractive but has not been reported yet.

In addition to detection and adsorption functions, the environmental friendliness, self-healing capability, recyclability, biodegradability, and biocompatibility of the designed hydrogels should also be considered. Since non-biodegradable hydrogels will create another waste disposal problem after the water treatment processes, the development of biodegradable hydrogels for heavy metal detection and removal is highly encouraged in order to conserve the environment. Gelatin, a sort of natural polymers with rich active groups suitable as hydrogel matrix to adsorb Cd2+ and Pb2+ [22], can emit violet fluorescence based on the existence of tyrosine, making it a potential standard fluorophore [23]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials with good hydrophilicity and biocompatibility, have been widely used as responsive fluorophores to detect metal ions including Cd2+ and Pb2+ [24,25]. The innovative combination of these two fluorophores can develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform with good biodegradability and biocompatibility for Cd2+ and Pb2+. Meanwhile, using polymers containing reversible dynamic bonds to synthesize gels with double network (DN) structures has been proved as an effective way to offer self-healabling capability, reprocessability and improved mechanical strength [26,27]. Thioctic acid (TA), as natural molecule in plants and animals with disulfide and carboxyl groups as active sites to adsorb Cd2+ and Pd2+ [28], can form self-healable supramolecular [29,30] based on dynamic bonds, and the obtained materials have been proved to be biodegradable and biocompatible [31]. Therefore, the combination of gelatin and poly(thioctic acid) (P(TA)) will not only endow the gel with excellent biodegradability and biocompatibility but also exceptional self-healing capability and reprocessability due to the existence of abundant dynamic bonds. Hydrogels that integrate those multi-functions and features can truly achieve longer service life, avoid the waste of resources, and prevent the secondary environmental pollution. To the best of our knowledge, no study has integrated these functions and features into a single gel platform so far.

This work aims at developing a novel multifunctional gel for sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection and effective adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water. With an elaborate design, glycidyl methacrylate functionalized fluorescent GQDs (GGQDs), N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), TA, and fluorescent gelatin were rationally selected to construct the P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN gel (Scheme 1 ). As mentioned above, TA and gelatin can give the gel with good biodegradability, biocompatibility, self-healing capability and reprocessability. MBA and GGQDs can quench the terminal diradicals to overcome the metastability of P(TA). GGQDs, with different fluorescent color from gelatin, can produce quantitative fluorescence “turn on/off” response to Cd2+/Pb2+. Meanwhile, all components can supply chelating groups (–COO-, –NH2, –CO-NH-, –OH, -S-S-), ensuring high adsorption capacity toward Cd2+ and Pb2+. After adsorbing Cd2+/Pb2+, the fluorescence of GGQDs can be significantly enhanced/quenched, while the fluorescence change of gelatin is relatively small, in which pattern the ratiometric fluorescent responses can help the gel to achieve simultaneous sensitive detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ using naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode. Meanwhile, owing to the synergistic effects of two natural polymer networks, porous structure, plentiful chelating groups and abundant dynamic bonds, the excellent adsorptivity, self-healing capability, reprocessability, biodegradability and biocompatibility can be achieved in a single gel system simultaneously. This is the first report of the gel that integrate those multi-functions and features for detection and adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water. We believe that this work will play an indispensable role for the development of versatile sensing and adsorption materials and their applications.

Glycidyl methacrylate, N,N'-methylenebisacrylamide, barium chloride dihydrate (99.99%), cadmium chloride (99.99%), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (99.99%), chromium(Ⅱ) chloride (99.9%), chromium(III) chloride (99.9%), potassium dichromate (99.8%), nickel chloride hexahydrate (99.9%), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (99.99%), zinc chloride (99.99%), copric chloride dihydrate (99.99%), iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (99.95%), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (99%), lead nitrate (99.99%), and thioctic acid were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Mercury bichloride (99.9%) was purchased from Shudu Chemical Company. Nitric acid, gelatin, phosphate buffer saline solution (pH = 7.4), sodium hydroxide, and other sodium salts of different anions were obtained from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Fetal bovine serum, Dulbecco's modified eagle medium, pancreatin, and penicillin–streptomycin, were purchased from Hyclone. Trypsin was purchased from Adamas-beta. Cell Counting Kit-8, 4ʹ,6ʹ-diamidino-2-phenylindole, propidium iodide were purchased from ApexBio Technology LLC. All commercially available chemicals were used as received.

GQDs with amino and carboxyl groups were prepared from graphite powder based on the method previously reported in our laboratory [32]. To synthesize the glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) functionalized GQDs (GGQDs), GMA was used as a vinylation agent to react with amine groups on the GQDs. Typically, dried GQDs (1 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of water, then 10 mL of GMA was added into the above GQDs solution and stirred at 30 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the oil phase of the solution was removed, and the water phase was further washed by n-hexane to remove the unreacted GMA molecules. Finally, the dried GGQDs was obtained by lyophilization of the remaining water solution. Yield = 1.248 g. Porosity (measured by a pycnometer [33]): 76%. FT-IR (KBr): 3600 ∼ 3000, 3000 ∼ 2800, 1766, 1716, 1600,1401 ∼ 1336, 1175, 1120, 947, 893, 833, 716, 618 cm−1; Average dimeter (TEM): 66.8 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN hydrogel and gel.
ce:section.ce:para: The P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN hydrogel and gel were prepared via a one pot procedure. In a typical preparation process, NaOH (0.191 g, 70 mol% of TA) was firstly dissolved in 16 mL deionized water containing TA (5.712 g) to obtain a transparent solution. Successively, MBA (0.240 g), gelatin (2.856 g) and GGQDs (0.032 g) were dissolved into the above solution. Then, the solution was transferred into the oven at 68 °C for 3 h. P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN hydrogel can be obtained when the viscous sol was cooled to room temperature. To get the P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN gel film, the hot sol obtained as mentioned above was poured into a Teflon mould with a length of 80 mm, a width of 80 mm and a height of 8 mm, and then placed in the open oven drying to constant weight. FT-IR (ATR): 3278, 3077, 2926, 2854, 1647, 1544, 1438, 1402, 1331, 1312, 1281, 1246, 1120, 1082, 1048 cm−1; Swelling ratio: 5260%.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of the powder samples were measured with a NICOLET iS10 spectrometer. The FT-IR spectra of gel films were measured via an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. UV–vis absorption spectra analyses were carried out using a UV-1900 spectrometer using a 1.0 cm optical path length quartz cells. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted utilizing an F-7100 spectrophotometer equipment. Time-correlated single photon counting time-resolved emission spectra (TCSPC-TRES) were recorded using a Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer. 1H NMR spectrum was measured on an AV II-600 (Bruker, 400 MHz) spectrometer. Differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) was determined using a DSC-204F (Netzsch) with a heating rate of 5 K min−1 under N2 protection. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GGQDs on carbon-coated copper grids was taken using a TecnaiG2F20S-TWIN transmission electron microscope operated at 100 kV. Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images of the lyophilized hydrogel, the lyophilized swolling gel, and the gel were observed with a JSM-7500F scanning electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV in the different magnification level. Before examination, the surface of the hydrogels was sprayed with a gold layer. The specific surface areas and pore structures of samples were determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) based on N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm at 77.3 K using an automatic surface and porosity analyzer (Micrometrics, ASAP 2460, USA). The surface compositions of the gel before and after adsorbing Cd2+ and Pd2+ were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an ultrahigh vacuum ESCAProbeP spectrometer with a monochromatic Al X-ray radiation source. Wide-scan surveys of all elements were performed, with subsequent high-resolution scans of the C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p, Cd3d and Pb4f peaks.

The detailed procedures for the swelling capacity characterizations, fluorescence sensing response measurements, batches adsorption studies, mechanical and self-healing measurements, remolding and reprocessing properties measurements, biodegradability test, and in vitro cytotoxicity assay are provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN gel
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs plays a crucial role for the gel to realize the detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+. GQDs containing amino and carboxyl groups showing both excitation- and concentration-dependent multicolor photoluminescence features have been reported in our previous work [32]. Herein, in order to covalently fixed GQDs into the gel, GGQD was further functionalized by glycidyl methacrylate to prepare polymerizable GGQDs (Scheme 1a). FT-IR characterizations demonstrate that GGQDs have been successfully prepared, and a lot of unreacted functional groups still remained on GGQDs, which can supply chelating sites for Cd2+/Pb2+ to achieve detection capability (see Fig. S1, for details). The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) indicates that GGQDs have an average diameter of 66.8 nm. PL emission and 3D fluorescence spectra of GGQDs solutions with different concentrations (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3) show that GGQDs have the same PL characteristics as GQDs. Fluorescence colors including blue, cyan, green, chartreuse, and yellow could be obtained by regulating the excitation wavelength/concentration of GGQDs (Fig. S4). The maximum emission peaks of GGQDs shifted from 449 nm to 545 nm as the concentration increases from 0.25 mg mL−1 to 16 mg mL−1 (Fig. 1b), covering the emission regions from blue to chartreuse (Fig. 1c). Under 365 nm excitation wavelength, the emission peaks can also cover the emission regions from blue to chartreuse (Fig. S5). As reported in our previous work, the red shift of the emission peaks with increasing concentration of GGQDs could be ascribed to aggregation-mediated energy levels reconstruction in GGQDs [32]. The characteristic of adjustable PL makes GGQDs an ideal probe for constructing ratiometric fluorescent sensor, thus the potential of GGQDs in sensing detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ were explored. As displayed in Fig. 1d, 3D fluorescence spectra present remarkable fluorescence enhancement/quenching in presence of 100 μM Cd2+/Pb2+, indicating that GGQDs could be used as fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for Cd2+ and “turn-off” sensor for Pb2+.

As far as we know, the mechanisms behind GQDs-based sensors toward different metal ions are still indistinct [25]. The 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1d), TCSPC-TRES, UV–vis and PL excitation spectra analysis (see, Fig. S6-S8 and Table S1, for details) indicated that the chelation of GGQDs and Cd2+/Pb2+ could be considered to explain fluorescent “turn-on/off”. The possible mechanisms behind the chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) and chelation quenched fluorescence (CHQF) for GGQDs + Cd2+/Pb2+ are proposed as shown in Fig. 1e. The interactions between the lone pair electrons of the nitrogen/oxygen and the vacant 5 s-orbital of Cd2+ can prevent the intramolecular electron transfer (IET) caused quenching, thus the radiative decay pathway and the PL intensity will be enhanced [34,35]. Moreover, since Cd2+ is diamagnetic ion with completely filled 4d-orbitals, it is unable for the excited electrons of GGQDs transfer to Cd2+. For Pb2+, although it can prevent the IET caused quenching, the remaining empty 6p-orbitals can accept the excited electrons of GGQDs, thus non-radiative electron transferring is believed to be the main reason for the CHQF [36].

Based on thermal-initiated ring-opening polymerization of TA to form P(TA) with terminal diradicals (Scheme 1b) and that gelatin can be easily heated in water to form a gel network, P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN hydrogel and gel were prepared via simple heating and drying procedures (Scheme 1c). Water soluble MBA together with GGQDs could quench the terminal diradicals by inverse vulcanization to overcome the undesired metastability of P(TA) [30]. 1H NMR, DSC, FT-IR characterizations determined the reaction temperature should be 68 °C and confirmed the role of MBA and GGQDs in terminal diradicals quenching (see, Fig. S9-S11, for details). Moreover, the optimized amounts of MBA and GGQDs were determined to be 4.2 wt% (wt%) of TA (see Fig. S12-S14, for details) and 2 mg mL−1 (see, Fig. S15-S16, for details) respectively to obtain a tough, transparent, and high water-swelling gel with strongest PL intensity.

Since the water in hydrogel is volatile, gel is considered to be easier to store than hydrogel. However, whether the gel can be swelled again to restore porous structure is extremely important, because the porous structure is believed to play an essential role in the transportation and adsorption of metal ions [37,38]. The microstructures of the hydrogel, gel and swelling gel were characterized by SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the lyophilized hydrogel presents a porous structure while the gel exhibits non-porous structure. The swelling ratio of the gel could reach 5260% (Fig. S17), indicating that the gel could adsorb a large amount of water, thus facilitating the regeneration of well-connected porous structure as shown in the SEM image of the lyophilized swelling gel. The specific surface areas and pore structures analyses of dry samples were performed and the obtained BET parameters are summarized in Table S2. BET specific surface areas for lyophilized hydrogel, gel and lyophilized swelling gel were 0.2016 m2 g−1, 0.1396 m2 g−1 and 0.2173 m2 g−1, respectively. The results demonstrated that the presence of 3D connected macroporous structures (as shown in the SEM images) in the lyophilized hydrogel and swelling gel could give them a larger specific surface area than that of the gel. Based on the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH), the average pore diameter for lyophilized hydrogel and swelling gel were 5.9857 nm and 3.4680 nm, respectively. The results indicated that in addition to the macropores, there are also mesopores, which also play a key role for the larger specific surface area in the lyophilized hydrogel and swelling gel. In contrast, no mesopore was found in the gel, which was also consistent with the SEM result.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the GGQDs solution (2 mg mL−1) exhibits green-emitting, which is distinct from that violet color of the gelatin solution (10 wt%) (Fig. 2c). The 3D fluorescence, UV–vis and PL excitation spectra of gelatin with and without 100 μM Cd2+/Pb2+ (see, Fig. S18, for details) indicated that CHEF and CHQF mechanisms were also applicable to gelatin + Cd2+/Pb2+. Notably, the effects of Cd2+ and Pb2+ on the fluorescence of gelatin are relatively small with respect to that of GGQDs. The weak CHEF and CHQF effects are ascribe to that oxygen with higher electronegativity shows stronger attraction between its nucleus and lone pair electrons than that of nitrogen, thus Cd2+ and Pb2+ are inclined to coordinate with nitrogen-containing groups rather than the hydroxyl group of tyrosine [16]. Therefore, by combining violet-emitting gelatin and green-emitting GGQDs into the gel, different ratiometric fluorescent responses of the gel to Cd2+ and Pb2+ could be achieved. PL emission spectra (Fig. 2d) show that the maximum emission peak of the prepared gel is located at 485 nm. The gel can emit cyan fluorescence, which is attributed to different colors emitted by gelatin and GGQDs (Fig. S19). 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2e and Fig. 2f) of GGQDs and gelatin demonstrate that they presented distinct fluorescence centers. And the fluorescent centers belong to gelatin and GGQDs can be found in the 3D fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2g) of the gel. The PL intensity of the gel was weaker than that of GGQDs/gelatin could be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) of P(TA-MBA) (Fig. S20). The PL intensity of GGQDs center is stronger than that of gelatin could also be explained by the fact that IFE of P(TA-MBA) on gelatin is stronger than that of GGQDs.

To explore the feasibility of the prepared gel in the detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+, the gel was first cut into gel discs (diameter: 11.00 mm, thickness: 1.00 mm, see Fig. S15) to adsorb Cd2+/Pb2+ in water (1200 μM, 200 μL). Then, 3D fluorescence spectra and related fluorescence photographs of the gel discs before and after adsorbing 0.24 μmol Cd2+/Pb2+ were recorded. As exhibited in Fig. 2h, the PL intensity of the gel increased after adsorbing Cd2+, while decreased after adsorbing Pb2+. Interestingly, the fluorescence color of the gel could change from cyan to chartreuse and violet after adsorbing Cd2+ and Pb2+, respectively. Significantly changes of the fluorescence centers, which is the dominant reason for the changes of fluorescence intensity and color of the gels, could be observed in the obtained 3D fluorescence spectra. The results confirmed that the prepared gel has excellent sensing detection capabilities toward Cd2+ and Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN gel
ce:section.ce:para:
To gain further insight into the capabilities of the gel for detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+, the fluorescence responses of gel discs after adsorbing various metal ions and different amounts of Cd2+/Pb2+ were investigated. First, the PL responses toward various kinds of common heavy metal ions, including Ba2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Cr2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+ were examined to reveal the selectivity of the gel. Fig. 3 a shows the photographs of gel discs after adsorbing 0.20 μmol different heavy metal ions under daylight and 365 nm UV light. The appearance color of the gel remarkably changed to reddish brown after adsorbing Pb2+, whereas no change was observed for gel discs after absorbing other metal ions. Unexpectedly, significant fluorescence color changes, which can be clearly distinguished by simple naked-eye observation, could only be observed for gel discs after adsorbing Cd2+ and Pb2+. Moreover, PL emission spectra (Fig. 3b) indicate that the most obvious fluorescent “turn-on” and “turn-off” effects can be achieved by Cd2+ and Pb2+, respectively. The relative quenching efficiency (RQE) values of gel discs were calculated based the PL intensity at 485 nm in Fig. 3b using the equation (1):
(1)
RQE =
(
I 0
- I )
I 0

where I and I0 are the PL intensity of gel discs with and without adsorption of metal ions, respectively. Fig. 3c shows the RQE values of gel discs after adsorbing Cd2+ and Pb2+ were −0.223 and 0.430, respectively. The results suggested that the gel has higher selectivity for detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ than other metal ions. The fluorescence responses of the gel to different anions were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S21, no obvious fluorescence response was observed, indicating that anion interference can be excluded. To verify the selectivity of the gel for practical detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+, the competition experiments were further conducted. As shown in Fig. S22, except when the Cd2+ and Pb2+ coexist, similar color changes and fluorescence responses of the gel can also be observed after adsorbing 0.20 μmol Cd2+/Pb2++0.20 μmol other competitive metal ions. However, considering that the effects of Cd2++Pb2+ on the appearance and fluorescence of the gel can be significantly distinguished from other Cd2++Mnn+ systems, it is also feasible for the gel to simultaneously detect Cd2+ and Pb2+. These results further identified that the gel exhibits a satisfactory selectivity toward Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection.

As shown in Fig. 3d, the appearance color of gels did not change after adsorbing Cd2+, and the fluorescence color of gels gradually changed from cyan to chartreuse as the amount of adsorbed Cd2+ increases. Moreover, the PL intensity of the gel at 485 nm increased as the amount of adsorbed Cd2+ increases (Fig. 3e), and good linearity between the RQE and the amount of adsorbed Cd2+ could be observed (Fig. 3f). However, as shown in Fig. 3g, the appearance color of gels after adsorbing Pb2+ gradually changed to darker reddish brown, and the fluorescence color of the gel gradually changed from cyan to violet as the amount of adsorbed Pb2+ increases. The PL intensity of the gel decreased as the amount of adsorbed Pb2+ increases (Fig. 3h), and good linearity between the RQE and the amount of adsorbed Pb2+ could be observed (Fig. 3i). These results indicated that selective naked-eye detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ was feasible, and the gel-based “turn-on” and “turn-off” sensors were sensitive to Cd2+ and Pb2+. Therefore, the gel-based sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity is suitable for detecting Cd2+ and Pb2+ through naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode in a broad linear range (0.02–0.24 μmol). The detection limits of Cd2+ and Pb2+ using the gel were determined to be 0.495 nmol and 0.251 nmol respectively based on three times signal-to-noise level of the blank sample (3σ/s, σ is the standard deviation of the blank for 10 replicates and s is the slope of linear plot between (I-I0) and the amount of adsorbed metal ion). The low detection limit values indicated that the gel would has good prospects in trace Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection applications. Notably, as shown in Table 1 , the gel developed in this work is the first time to realize simultaneous sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water, making the gel available for on-site monitoring Cd2+ and Pb2+ polluted water.

To investigate the reusability of the gel, recycle detection tests were carried out. The results indicated that the gel possesses good reversibility and reusability for Cd2+ and Pb2+ in five cycles (Fig. S23). The excellent selectivity combined with high sensitivity and reusability suggest that the prepared gel could be directly applied for detecting Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water sample. The gel therefore was applied for the detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in Jinjiang river (Table S3 shows the quality parameters of the river water). As shown in Fig. S24, the PL intensity of the gel increases as the amount of Cd2+ increases and the PL intensity of the gel decreases as the amount of Pb2+ increases. Surprisingly good linear relationship between the RQE and the amount of Cd2+/Pb2+ in the range of 0.04–0.20 μmol could also be observed. The determination results (Table S4) show that the recoveries were found to be in the range of 87.5–115.0%. The above results indicated that, despite the possible presence of numerous organic/ionic interferences, the prepared gel exhibited excellent applicability for detecting Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real water samples, suggesting its great potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in water
ce:section.ce:para: As the chemical activities of metal ions and the ionization of the functional groups in the gel are closely related to the pH [37], the effects of the solution pH on the adsorption capacities of the gel for Cd2+ and Pb2+ were explored. Different pH solutions were deployed from 2.0 to 6.0 for Cd2+ and from 2.0 to 5.0 for Pb2+ due to appearing a distinct white turbidity when pH ≥ 6 [44]. The adsorption capacities of the gel increased as pH increases from 2.0 to 5.0 (Fig. 4 a). At low pH conditions, excessive H+ may compete with Cd2+/Pb2+ for adsorption sites, and amino groups in the gel could be protonated to hamper the adsorption of Cd2+/Pb2+. Therefore, the initial pH of the Cd2+/Pb2+ solution was controlled at 5.0 for further experiment. Fig. 4b shows the effects of adsorption time on the adsorption capacities of the gel. Obviously, the adsorption capacities of the gel increased rapidly within initial 180 min, and the adsorption could reach equilibrium within 300 min. Within the initial 180 min, the gel could rapidly reach swelling equilibrium state (Fig. S17), and water diffusion-facilitated porous structure will provide transportation channels for Cd2+/Pb2+. Meanwhile, water can act as carrier of Cd2+/Pb2+ during the swelling process, allowing Cd2+/Pb2+ to smoothly penetrate into the gel and be adsorbed by the internal active sites. Due to there were enough active sites for Cd2+/Pb2+ at this stage, the adsorption capacity of the gel showed a rapidly increase [45]. The relationships between the initial concentration of Cd2+/Pb2+ and the adsorption capacity of the gel indicated that higher adsorption capacity could be achieved with a high initial concentration (Fig. 4c), which might be attributed to that the chance of collision between Cd2+/Pb2+ and adsorption sites would increase as the initial concentration increases [46].

Different kinetic and isotherm models were adopted to explore the related adsorption behavior and mechanism. Fig. 4d and Fig. 4e exhibit the linear fitting results of pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model, respectively. The correlation coefficients (Table S5) of the pseudo-second-order model were higher than those obtained with the pseudo-first-order model, indicating that chemical reaction between Cd2+/Pb2+ and the gel was the main adsorption behavior [47]. Fig. 4f shows the linear fitting results of intraparticle diffusion model, and the plots of qt versus t1/2 included two-section linearity (ki1 > ki2) (Table S6), indicating two consecutive stages took place during the adsorption process. The first was the surface adsorption stage, in which Cd2+/Pb2+ was adsorbed on the surface of the gel. After the surface adsorption reached saturation, the adsorbed ions would gradually diffuse from surface to inside of the gel at the second stage [48].

The fitting results demonstrated that the Langmuir model (Fig. 4g) is more suitable than the Freundlich model (Fig. 4h) for describing the adsorption process, which suggests that monolayer coverage of Cd2+/Pb2+ on the surface of the gel is the main adsorption mechanism [47]. The maximum adsorption capacities of the gel for Cd2+ and Pb2+ obtained by Langmuir linear fitting (Table S7) were 194.553 mg g−1 and 259.067 mg g−1, respectively. A pertinent comparison of various pertinent gel (Table 2 ) and non-gel (Table S8) adsorbent materials featuring Cd2+ and Pb2+ adsorption capabilities were completed. Obviously, the adsorption capacities of the developed gel in this work toward Cd2+ and Pb2+ surpassed these reported adsorbent materials.

Reusability of adsorbents is an important factor to evaluate their potential economic values for practical applications. To assess its reusability, the gel discs adsorbed Cd2+/Pb2+ were regenerated by using 0.1 M HNO3 solution and 0.1 M NaOH solutions. Then, the desorbed gel discs were rinsed with deionized water, dried until constant weight and re-applied against Cd2+/Pb2+ solution. The consecutive adsorption–desorption process was performed for five cycles, and the adsorption capacities of the regenerated gel are presented in Fig. 4i. The adsorption capacities of the gel for Cd2+ and Pb2+ decreased from 99.6% to 83.7% and 96.0% to 88.3%, respectively, indicating that the regenerated gel still could effectively adsorb Cd2+ and Pb2+ after several cycles of reuse.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-healing and reprocessing performances of the P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN gel
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the prepared gel will inevitably be destroyed by external forces during practical detection and adsorption applications, the self-healing and reprocessing performances of the gel are particularly important, which can greatly extend its service life. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a and Video S1, the cut circular hydrogel disk could be autonomously and quickly (several minutes) healed at room temperature (RT, 25 °C), indicating it possesses good self-healing capability. The stress–strain curve (Fig. 5b) shows that the hydrogel gained 92% in stress and 89% in strain after 10 min of healing (Fig. S25a). As shown in Fig. 5c, the prepared gel also manifested excellent self-healing capability at RT, and the healed gel can sustain folding, twisting, and stretching (Video S2 and Video S3). Both the stress and strain increased as the healing time increases (Fig. 5d), and the healing efficiency could reach up to 92% in stress and 98% in strain (Fig. S25b) after 24 h.

In addition to self-healing capability, the prepared hydrogel could be reshaped into desired shapes (e.g. petal-like, see Fig. 5a) by reheating the hydrogel to 68 °C and then cooling to RT. As presented in Fig. 5e, the gel fragments can be reswelled into hydrogel fragments in water, and flowable sol can also be formed by heating the hydrogel fragments to 68 °C. When the sol was cooled to room temperature, a recycled hydrogel with desired shape could be obtained. Meanwhile, the sol could be dried to obtain a new recycled gel film. The mechanical properties of the gel could be well stored after three reprocessing cycles (Fig. 5f), and the stress and strain could recover more than 96% and 93%, respectively (Fig. S25c). Besides, the 3D fluorescence spectra of recycled gels showed no remarkable changes in fluorescent center and PL intensity (Fig. 5g), indicating that the recycled gel can be re-used without sacrificing its original properties and functions.

The possible self-healing mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 5h, during the self-healing period, the dynamic hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions first formed a weak interface by noncovalent interactions, and then the dynamic covalent exchange of the broken disulfide bonds accelerated the formation of strong interface by covalent bonding [30]. The heat-mediated remolding and reprocessing mechanisms are proposed as shown in Fig. 5i. When the swelling gel fragments are heated to 68 °C, both dynamic covalent crosslinked P(TA-MBA-GGQDs) network and physical crosslinked gelatin network will be collapsed due to the breakage of disulfide bonds [29,30] and the helix-to-coil transition of the gelatin chains [61]. Therefore, the swelling gel fragments undergo a phase change from gel to sol. When the temperature of the sol is cooled to RT, the double network structure will be reformed again due to the recombination of the broken disulfide bonds and the reformation of the physical helical junction net points.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodegradability and biocompatibility of the P(TA-MBA-GGQDs)/Ge DN gel
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the biodegradability of the gel, in vitro enzymatic hydrolysis tests were carried out in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) at 37 °C with pancreatin [62]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the gel in PBS solution with pancreatin became gradually smaller as time increases, but the gel in PBS solution has not changed. The gel can be completely degraded after six day of enzymatic degradation (Fig. 6b), which might ascribe to both P(TA-MBA-GGQDs) and gelatin networks are biodegradable [31,63]. The potential toxicity of GGQDs and gel were investigated using live/dead (4ʹ,6ʹ-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI)/propidium iodide (PI) staining) assay. DAPI enters both living and dead cells, showing blue-emission, whereas PI only stains dead cells with an orange-red signal. Only a few HeLa cells were stained with orange-red fluorescence (Fig. 6c), indicating that the prepared GGQDs and gel had good cytocompatibility. Moreover, the cell viability assays (CCK-8) demonstrated that more than 92% of cells survived after being incubated with GGQDs/gel extract at concentration up to 400 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6d), further implying their excellent biocompatibility. Good biodegradability and biocompatibility indicated that the gel will not cause secondary contamination in practical applications. To display the advantages of the gel, the gel is comprehensively compared with previously reported gel-based adsorbent materials. As shown in Fig. 6e, none of the existing studies has integrated the above-mentioned functions and features into a single gel-based absorbent.

To reveal the adsorption mechanisms, XPS characterizations were conducted. The presence of new peaks for Cd and Pb in survey spectra (Fig. 7 a) verified that Cd2+ and Pb2+ have been adsorbed on the gel [7,64]. Peaks in N 1 s (Fig. 7b) and O 1 s spectra (Fig. 7c) of the gel are associated with C-N (399.46 eV), N–H (399.96 eV), O–H (530.86 eV), C = O (531.56 eV), and C-O (532.21 eV) [19,65]. The shift of these peaks is due to the coordination between nitrogen atoms and Cd2+/Pb2+, the electrostatic interactions between –COO-and Cd2+/Pb2+ and coordination between oxygen atoms and Cd2+/Pb2+ [66]. Peaks in S 2p spectrum (Fig. 7d) of the gel are attributed to C-S bond (S 2p3/2 (162.96 eV) and S 2p1/2 (163.36 eV)) and S-S bond (S2p3/2 (164.16 eV) and S2p1/2 (164.56 eV)) [67]. The shift of these peaks indicated that disulfide bonds can chelate with Cd2+ and Pb2+ [68]. Moreover, the presence of new peaks attributed to Cd-S and Pb-S proved that disulfide bond can also react with Cd2+/Pb2+ to form covalent bonds as shown below [6,67]:
(2)
R - S - S - R ⇌ R - S H + H S - R
(3)
R - S H +
M
2 +
+ H S - R ⇌ R - S - M - S - R + 2
H
+

Stronger Cd-S peaks indicated that Cd2+ react more easily with disulfide bond, which agrees with the Pearson’s soft acid–soft base predictions, as thiol group is a soft base ligand with high affinity for soft acid (Cd2+) [69]. Pb2+, a borderline acid, has medium affinity to thiol group [70]. Moreover, the Cd 3d and Pb 4f (Fig. S26) spectra can be divided into three peaks, indicating that nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur atoms participated in the chelation of Cd2+ and Pb2+ [71].

Based on the above results, the possible mechanisms behind the adsorption and the dual-mode detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ using the gel are proposed as shown in Scheme 2 . Concretely, the electron rich carboxyl, amine, amide, hydroxyl groups and disulfide bonds tend to donate electrons to electron deficient Cd2+/Pb2+ with vacant 5 s-orbital/6p-orbitals. Therefore, chelation occurred between Cd2+/Pb2+ and active groups of the gel enabled the effective adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+. The fluorescence sensing of the gel towards Cd2+ is attributed to CHEF. The PL intensities of GGQD and gelatin will increase after chelating with Cd2+, which directly lead to the increasement in PL intensity of the gel. Due to Cd2+ is facile to chelate with nitrogen-containing ligands, the CHEF effect of Cd2+ on GGQDs is thought to be far stronger than that of gelatin. The gel thus mainly presents the fluorescence color of GGQDs, which is chartreuse rather than green due to the relative concentration of GGQDs will increase with the drying process (Fig. S5). As for Pb2+, it can react with disulfide bonds to form sulfides with darker color (Fig. S27), and the UV–vis and PL spectra analysis indicated that only Pb2+ could further increase the IFE of P(TA-MBA) (see Fig. S28, for details). Therefore, the fluorescence sensing of the gel towards Pb2+ is attributed to IFE and CHQF. The fluorescence of the gel can be quenched by the enhanced IFE of P(TA-MBA). Moreover, due to the CHQF effect of Pb2+ on GGQDs is far stronger than that of gelatin, thus the gel presents the fluorescence color of gelatin.

In summary, for the first time we have successfully developed a novel multifunctional gel with high selectivity and sensitivity for simultaneous sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in a broad linear range (0.02–0.24 μmol). The gel exhibited cyan to chartreuse/violet fluorescence color change and fluorescent turn-on/turn-off switch after adsorbing Cd2+/Pb2+, which was achieved by innovative combination of GQDs and gelatin fluorophores based on CHEF/CHQF mechanism. The adsorption capacities of the gel toward Cd2+ and Pb2+ reached up to 194.553 and 259.067 mg g−1, respectively, benefiting from the porous structure and plentiful chelating groups brought by all components. The gel presented good reusability after five detection- and adsorption-regeneration cycles, and it also showed excellent applicability for detecting Cd2+ and Pb2+ in real river water samples, indicating it has great practical application potential. In addition, the gel exhibited exceptional self-healing capability, reprocessability, biodegradability and biocompatibility owing to the synergistic effects of two natural polymer networks and abundant reversible dynamic bonds, which make it fully recycled without sacrificing its original functions after being destroyed and unnecessary to worry about secondary contamination when it was completely discarded in practical applications. This work thus delivers an innovative design strategy for the simultaneous sensitive naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode detection and effective adsorption of Cd2+ and Pb2+, paving the way towards novel versatile functional materials for monitoring and remediation of heavy metal contamination.
